section.title: The Fall of Jerusalem Recounted
section.sectionRationale: The closing chapter of Jeremiah is a historical appendix, largely paralleling 2 Kings 24:18-25:30, that confirms in sober narrative everything the prophet had spoken. Zedekiah's rebellion, the siege, the burning of the temple, and the exile all happened exactly as declared. Yet the very last scene lifts the book's eyes forward: Jehoiachin, a captive descendant of David, is raised from prison and given a seat of honor in Babylon, a faint but real note of hope for the line the LORD had promised would never fail.
section.thesis: Every word Jeremiah spoke against Jerusalem came true in exact and painful detail, vindicating the prophet; yet the chapter closes by lifting up a son of David from prison, a small, deliberate sign that the LORD's promise to David's house was not, after all, destroyed.
section.frameNote: Six movements: Zedekiah's reign and rebellion; the siege and fall of Jerusalem; the burning of the temple, palace, and city; the temple's treasures carried to Babylon; the officials and people executed and exiled; and Jehoiachin lifted from prison, a note of hope for David's line.

units.label: Zedekiah's reign and rebellion
units.note: evil in the sight of the LORD

verses.text: Zedekiah was twenty-one years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem eleven years. His mother’s name was Hamutal daughter of Jeremiah; she was from Libnah.
And Zedekiah did evil in the sight of the LORD, just as Jehoiakim had done.
For because of the anger of the LORD, all this happened in Jerusalem and Judah, until He finally banished them from His presence. And Zedekiah also rebelled against the king of Babylon.

units.label: The siege and fall of Jerusalem

verses.text: So in the ninth year of Zedekiah’s reign, on the tenth day of the tenth month, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon marched against Jerusalem with his entire army. They encamped outside the city and built a siege wall all around it.
And the city was kept under siege until King Zedekiah’s eleventh year.
By the ninth day of the fourth month, the famine in the city was so severe that the people of the land had no food.

Then the city was breached; and though the Chaldeans had surrounded the city, all the men of war fled the city by night by way of the gate between the two walls near the king’s garden. They headed toward the Arabah,
but the army of the Chaldeans pursued the king and overtook Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho, and all his army was separated from him.
The Chaldeans seized the king and brought him up to the king of Babylon at Riblah in the land of Hamath, where he pronounced judgment on Zedekiah.
There at Riblah the king of Babylon slaughtered the sons of Zedekiah before his eyes, and he also killed all the officials of Judah.
Then he put out Zedekiah’s eyes, bound him with bronze shackles, and took him to Babylon, where he kept him in custody until his dying day.

units.label: The burning of the temple, palace, and city

verses.text: On the tenth day of the fifth month, in the nineteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar’s reign over Babylon, Nebuzaradan captain of the guard, a servant of the king of Babylon, entered Jerusalem.
He burned down the house of the LORD, the royal palace, and all the houses of Jerusalem, every significant building.
And the whole army of the Chaldeans under the captain of the guard broke down all the walls around Jerusalem.

Then Nebuzaradan captain of the guard carried into exile some of the poorest people and those who remained in the city, along with the deserters who had defected to the king of Babylon and the rest of the craftsmen.
But Nebuzaradan captain of the guard left behind some of the poorest of the land to tend the vineyards and fields.

units.label: The temple's treasures carried to Babylon
units.note: the bronze Sea and the two pillars

verses.text: Moreover, the Chaldeans broke up the bronze pillars and stands and the bronze Sea in the house of the LORD, and they carried all the bronze to Babylon.
They also took away the pots, shovels, wick trimmers, sprinkling bowls, dishes, and all the articles of bronze used in the temple service.
The captain of the guard also took away the basins, censers, sprinkling bowls, pots, lampstands, pans, and drink offering bowls, anything made of pure gold or fine silver.

As for the two pillars, the Sea, the twelve bronze bulls under it, and the movable stands that King Solomon had made for the house of the LORD, the weight of the bronze from all these articles was beyond measure.
Each pillar was eighteen cubits tall and twelve cubits in circumference; each was hollow, four fingers thick.
The bronze capital atop one pillar was five cubits high, with a network of bronze pomegranates all around. The second pillar, with its pomegranates, was similar.
Each capital had ninety-six pomegranates on the sides, and a total of a hundred pomegranates were above the surrounding network.

units.label: The officials and people executed and exiled
units.note: Judah taken from her own land

verses.text: The captain of the guard also took away Seraiah the chief priest, Zephaniah the priest of second rank, and the three doorkeepers.

Of those still in the city, he took a court official who had been appointed over the men of war, as well as seven trusted royal advisers. He also took the scribe of the captain of the army, who had enlisted the people of the land, and sixty men who were found in the city.
Nebuzaradan captain of the guard took them and brought them to the king of Babylon at Riblah.
There at Riblah in the land of Hamath, the king of Babylon struck them down and put them to death. So Judah was taken into exile, away from its own land.
These are the people Nebuchadnezzar carried away: in the seventh year, 3,023 Jews;
in Nebuchadnezzar’s eighteenth year, 832 people from Jerusalem;
in Nebuchadnezzar’s twenty-third year, Nebuzaradan captain of the guard carried away 745 Jews. So in all, 4,600 people were taken away.

units.label: Jehoiachin lifted from prison
units.note: a faint hope for David's line

verses.text: On the twenty-fifth day of the twelfth month of the thirty-seventh year of the exile of Jehoiachin king of Judah, in the first year of the reign of Evil-merodach king of Babylon, he {{nasa|pardoned}} Jehoiachin king of Judah and released him from prison.
And he spoke kindly to Jehoiachin and set his throne above the thrones of the other kings who were with him in Babylon.
So Jehoiachin changed out of his prison clothes, and he dined regularly at the king’s table for the rest of his life.
And the king of Babylon provided Jehoiachin a daily portion for the rest of his life, until the day of his death.

nasa.gloss: Literally to lift up the head, a Hebrew idiom for restoring someone to favor or honor, used here of Jehoiachin's release, and earlier of Pharaoh restoring his cupbearer, Genesis 40:13. Behind the plain word pardoned lies this vivid picture: a captive son of David, once bowed low, has his head lifted again.

basic.context: Jeremiah's final chapter steps back from prophecy into sober historical narrative, largely repeating 2 Kings 24:18-25:30, to confirm in fact everything the prophet had spoken for forty years. Zedekiah's rebellion brings Nebuchadnezzar's siege; the city falls to famine and breach; Zedekiah is captured and blinded after watching his sons killed; the temple and city are burned; the temple's furnishings are carried to Babylon; officials are executed; and the population is counted out into exile. Yet the very last scene turns unexpectedly toward hope: Jehoiachin, a captive descendant of David, is lifted from prison and seated in honor at the Babylonian king's table.
basic.hermeneutics: This is straightforward historical narrative, not poetic oracle, recording events that actually happened in 588-586 BC and again in 561 BC. It functions as the book's vindication: everything Jeremiah warned Judah's kings and people about across decades of resistance and denial came to pass exactly as spoken. The abiding truth is not merely that judgment fell, but that the LORD's word, however long resisted, proves utterly reliable, for judgment and, in Jehoiachin's case, for a hidden thread of mercy as well.
basic.originalLanguages: The idiom behind pardoned in verse 31 is nasa rosh, to lift up the head, the very phrase used when Pharaoh restored his cupbearer to honor, Genesis 40:13. Its use here for a captive son of David, decades into exile, is not incidental; it signals that the line of David, though bowed to the ground, has not been erased.
basic.typology: Jehoiachin lifted from prison and seated above the other captive kings prefigures the greater exaltation of David's greater Son: Christ, humbled to the point of death, is lifted up and seated far above every throne, Philippians 2:8-9, Ephesians 1:20-21. A humbled descendant of David raised to a place of honor in a foreign court becomes a small but real sign that the promise of 2 Samuel 7:16, that David's house would endure forever, was still alive even in exile's darkest hour.
basic.christ: The chapter that records Jerusalem's total devastation and the apparent end of the Davidic monarchy closes instead with a Davidic king lifted from prison and given a seat of honor, verses 31 to 34. That single scene keeps alive, right at Jeremiah's close, the promise the whole book has been building toward: a righteous Branch will spring up for David, 23:5-6, 33:14-17. Jehoiachin's small elevation is not that fulfillment, but it is the first sign that David's line survives exile, a lineage Matthew 1:11-12 traces directly to Christ Himself, the true and greater King who is lifted up not merely to a foreign king's table but to the right hand of God.
basic.god: God's word proves itself entirely reliable in this chapter: every warning Jeremiah gave came true in exact and painful historical detail, showing that the LORD does not speak carelessly and does not fail to act. Yet the same faithfulness that carried out judgment also preserved, against every appearance, the promised line of David, a mercy folded into the very last verses of the book.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 25:27-30 gives the nearly identical account this chapter draws from. 2 Samuel 7:12-16 records the original promise that David's throne would endure forever. Matthew 1:11-12 traces Christ's genealogy through Jeconiah, that is, Jehoiachin, showing this preserved line reaching all the way to the Messiah. Philippians 2:8-9 describes Christ humbled to death and then highly exalted, the pattern glimpsed faintly in Jehoiachin's own lifting up.
basic.oneStory: Jeremiah's closing chapter proves that every word of judgment the prophet spoke came true, and yet ends by lifting a son of David out of prison, a small, deliberate sign that the promise to David's house survived even total national catastrophe. That thread runs unbroken through the exile to Christ, the greater Son of David, humbled far more deeply than Jehoiachin and exalted far higher, in whom every promise of Jeremiah's book finds its yes and amen.

context.literary: A historical appendix, almost identical to 2 Kings 24:18-25:30, appended to close the book of Jeremiah with documentary confirmation of the prophet's decades of warning, moving from Zedekiah's reign, through the siege and fall, the plunder of the temple, and the numbering of exiles, before ending on the surprising note of Jehoiachin's release.
context.historical: Zedekiah's rebellion against Babylon led to Nebuchadnezzar's siege beginning in January 588 BC, the city's fall by famine and breach in the summer of 586 BC, the burning of the temple and city about a month later, and the final numbered deportations extending to 582 BC. The chapter's closing scene, Jehoiachin's release, occurred in 561 BC under Nebuchadnezzar's successor Evil-merodach, some twenty-five years after Jerusalem's fall.
context.canonical: This chapter's near duplicate in 2 Kings 25 shows the Former Prophets and the Latter Prophets converging to certify the same history; its closing scene of Jehoiachin's elevation becomes the historical anchor for tracing David's line through the exile into the genealogies of Christ, Matthew 1:11-12, 1 Chronicles 3:17-19.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To close the book of Jeremiah with sober historical confirmation that every warning the prophet spoke came to pass in detail, while preserving, in Jehoiachin's release, evidence that the LORD's promise to David's house had not been extinguished by the exile.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter narrates specific historical events; it prescribes no ritual or pattern to imitate. Its abiding message is that God's spoken word, whether warning or promise, is always kept, in its own time and way.

debates.question: Why does the book of Jeremiah end with Jehoiachin's release rather than with a clearer word of restoration for Judah as a whole?

views.label: A deliberate, understated sign of hope
views.case: By ending on a captive Davidic king lifted from prison rather than a triumphant national return, the book models the modest, patient hope Jeremiah himself preached throughout exile, trust that God keeps His promises even when their fulfillment looks small and incomplete, 29:10-14.

views.label: A historical fact included for its own value, with theological weight added by later revelation
views.case: The chapter may simply record what in fact happened to the last Davidic king in living memory, without intending a fully developed theological statement; its significance for the preservation of David's line becomes clear only in light of later Scripture, such as Matthew's genealogy.

debates.bearing: Either way, the placement of this detail at the very close of Jeremiah has been read across the canon as a sign that David's dynasty, though humbled, was preserved for the coming of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The idiom nasa rosh, to lift the head, lies behind the plain word pardoned in verse 31, the same phrase used when Pharaoh restored his cupbearer to honor in Genesis 40:13. Applied to a captive son of David near the end of his forty-year imprisonment, it carries weight beyond a simple release: a bowed head, and with it a bowed dynasty, is lifted again.

typology.type: Jehoiachin lifted from prison and seated above the other captive kings
typology.fulfillment: Christ, David's greater Son, humbled to the point of death and then highly exalted and seated far above every throne, Philippians 2:8-9, Ephesians 1:20-21.

study.christ: The chapter that records Jerusalem's total devastation and the apparent end of the Davidic monarchy closes instead with a Davidic king lifted from prison and given a seat of honor, verses 31 to 34. That single scene keeps alive, right at Jeremiah's close, the promise the whole book has been building toward: a righteous Branch will spring up for David, 23:5-6, 33:14-17. Jehoiachin's small elevation is not that fulfillment, but it is the first sign that David's line survives exile, a lineage Matthew 1:11-12 traces directly to Christ Himself, the true and greater King who is lifted up not merely to a foreign king's table but to the right hand of God.
study.god: God's word proves itself entirely reliable in this chapter: every warning Jeremiah gave came true in exact and painful historical detail, showing that the LORD does not speak carelessly and does not fail to act. Yet the same faithfulness that carried out judgment also preserved, against every appearance, the promised line of David, a mercy folded into the very last verses of the book.

crossReferences.note: The nearly identical account this chapter draws from, confirming the historical record of Jehoiachin's release.
The original promise that David's throne and kingdom would endure forever.
Traces Christ's genealogy through Jeconiah, that is, Jehoiachin, showing this preserved line reaching to the Messiah.
Christ humbled to the point of death and then highly exalted, the pattern glimpsed faintly in Jehoiachin's own lifting up.

study.oneStory: Jeremiah's closing chapter proves that every word of judgment the prophet spoke came true, and yet ends by lifting a son of David out of prison, a small, deliberate sign that the promise to David's house survived even total national catastrophe. That thread runs unbroken through the exile to Christ, the greater Son of David, humbled far more deeply than Jehoiachin and exalted far higher, in whom every promise of Jeremiah's book finds its yes and amen.

quiz.q: What triggered Nebuchadnezzar's final siege of Jerusalem, according to verse 3?

options.text: Zedekiah's rebellion against the king of Babylon.
options.note: Verse 3 states plainly that Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon, prompting the siege.

options.text: A famine that had nothing to do with Judah's kings.
options.note: The famine, verse 6, was a result of the siege, not its cause.

options.text: An unprovoked Babylonian invasion.
options.note: The text specifically names Zedekiah's rebellion as the trigger, not an unprovoked attack.

options.text: A trade dispute between Egypt and Babylon.
options.note: Egypt does not feature in this chapter's account of the siege's cause; Zedekiah's own rebellion is named directly.

quiz.q: What happened to Zedekiah after his capture at Jericho, verses 9 to 11?

options.text: His sons were killed before his eyes, then he was blinded and taken to Babylon in shackles.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 describe exactly this sequence of judgment on Zedekiah.

options.text: He was immediately executed.
options.note: The text describes his blinding and imprisonment, not execution.

options.text: He was restored to his throne under Babylonian oversight.
options.note: The opposite occurred; Zedekiah lost his throne, his sight, and his freedom.

options.text: He was exiled to Egypt.
options.note: He was taken to Babylon, not Egypt, and kept in custody there until his death.

quiz.q: What happened to the temple's bronze and gold articles, verses 17 to 20?

options.text: They were broken up or carried away to Babylon, their weight in bronze beyond measure.
options.note: Verses 17 to 20 detail the temple's furnishings being stripped and taken to Babylon.

options.text: They were buried in Jerusalem for safekeeping.
options.note: No such burial is recorded here; the text describes the articles being taken to Babylon.

options.text: They were left untouched by the Chaldeans.
options.note: The opposite; the chapter details their systematic removal.

options.text: They were melted down and reused to rebuild the temple immediately.
options.note: Not what the text describes; the articles were taken away, not reused on site.

quiz.q: What happens to Jehoiachin at the very end of the chapter, verses 31 to 34?

options.text: He is released from prison, spoken to kindly, and given a seat of honor at the Babylonian king's table.
options.note: Verses 31 to 34 describe this unexpected elevation after thirty-seven years of imprisonment.

options.text: He is executed alongside the other officials.
options.note: The opposite; he is released and honored, not executed.

options.text: He escapes from Babylon and returns to Jerusalem.
options.note: Not what the text describes; he remains in Babylon but is honored there.

options.text: He is forgotten and dies unnoticed in prison.
options.note: The opposite; the text specifically records his release and honored treatment for the rest of his life.

quiz.q: Why does Jehoiachin's release matter for the promise God made to David?

options.text: It shows David's line, though humbled, was preserved through exile, a lineage traced to Christ in Matthew's genealogy.
options.note: Matthew 1:11-12 traces Christ's ancestry through Jeconiah, that is, Jehoiachin, confirming the significance of his preservation.

options.text: It proves God's promise to David had already failed completely.
options.note: The opposite; Jehoiachin's survival and honor is precisely what shows the promise was not extinguished.

options.text: It has no connection to any promise about David's line.
options.note: Scripture draws this connection directly through the genealogies leading to Christ.

options.text: It means Jehoiachin himself was the promised Messiah.
options.note: Not the claim of the text; Jehoiachin's release is a sign of preservation, not itself the fulfillment, which comes in Christ.

questions: Every warning Jeremiah gave for decades came true in exact detail in this chapter. Where has God's word proven reliable in your own life, even when it took far longer than you expected to see it?
Zedekiah's rebellion, one man's decision, brought devastation on an entire city. Where might your own choices be affecting more people than you realize?

The temple's treasures, once signs of God's presence and glory, were carried off and broken up. Have you ever had to grieve the loss of something that once represented God's presence to you, and how did you find Him again afterward?
Jehoiachin spent thirty-seven years in prison before being lifted up; the promise to David's line looked dead for a generation. Where are you waiting for God to act on a promise that feels forgotten?

The chapter's last scene is small and easy to miss, a captive king given a better seat at a foreign table, yet Scripture treats it as significant. Where might God be doing something meaningful in your life that looks small or easy to overlook?
David's line survived total catastrophe to bring forth Christ. How does this shape your confidence that God can bring good, even from the darkest chapters of your own story?

translationNotes.note: Poetic or run-on line breaks in the source text have been joined into running verse text per house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
Poetic or run-on line breaks in the source text have been joined into running verse text per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Jeremiah 52:1-34.
